| Compa-Ratio | CR | 1.00 | ★★★★★ |
| Range Spread | RS | 25% to 60% | ★★★★☆ |
| Midpoint Progression | MP | 2.5% to 3.5% | ★★★★☆ |
| Real Wage Index | RWI | > 100 (rising) | ★★★☆☆ |
| Halsey Bonus | HB | 50% of time saved | ★★★★★ |
| Rowan Bonus | RB | Proportional | ★★★★☆ |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

These formulas and processes are the **backbone of every HR department** in India. For example:
- **TCS, Infosys, and Wipro** use Compa-Ratio to assess whether an employee is paid below, at, or above the market midpoint.
- **Manufacturing firms** like Tata Steel use Halsey and Rowan plans to incentivize direct workers on the shop floor.
- **Public sector banks** use the **10th Bipartite Settlement** formula to revise wages for lakhs of employees.
- **Startups** like Flipkart and Ola use **market pricing** + **pay bands** to attract scarce tech talent.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever a wage question appears, always mention **Internal Equity + External Competitiveness + Individual Performance** — these three are the *trinity* the examiner looks for.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Wage Determination Process (Expanded)

Let us walk through the process with the **rigor of a KTU 14-mark answer**.

#### **Step 1: Conduct Job Analysis**

**Definition:** Job analysis is the systematic collection and recording of information about job duties, responsibilities, necessary skills, outcomes, and work environment.

**Activities:**
- Identify key jobs (typically **benchmark jobs** representing **70–80%** of the workforce).
- Use **methods**: interviews, structured questionnaires, observation, work diaries, and the **critical incident technique**.
- Document outputs into a **Job Description** and **Job Specification**.

**Deliverables:**
$$
\text{Job Analysis Output} = \text{Job Description} + \text{Job Specification}
$$

#### **Step 2: Develop a Job Evaluation Plan**

Choose a job evaluation method based on:
- Number of jobs
- Budget available
- Complexity
- Need for defensibility

The four classical methods are:

$$
\text{Job Evaluation Methods} = \{\text{Ranking, Classification, Point Method, Factor Comparison}\}
$$

#### **Step 3: Apply the Chosen Method**

For the **Point Method** (most commonly tested), the steps are:
1. Identify **compensable factors** (typically 4 to 10).
2. Define **degrees** for each factor.
3. Assign **weights (points)** to each degree.
4. Sum up points for each job.

**Mathematical Expression:**
$$
\text{Total Job Points} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Weight}_i \times \text{Degree Assigned}_i)
$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of compensable factors (typically **5 to 10**)
- $\text{Weight}_i$ = points assigned to the chosen degree of factor $i$

#### **Step 4: Conduct Wage/Salary Survey**

**Tools used:**
- Published salary surveys (e.g., Aon Hewitt, Mercer Towers Watson).
- Government data (Bureau of Labor Statistics equivalent in India: **Labour Bureau, Ministry of Labour**).
- Direct competitor benchmarking.

**Mathematical aggregation of survey data:**
$$
\text{Median Market Rate (P50)} = \text{Middle value when arranged in ascending order}
$$

$$
\text{Market Average} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Company}_i \text{ Pay}}{k}
$$

Where $k$ = number of surveyed companies (typically **15 to 25** for reliability).

#### **Step 5: Determine Wage Curve**

Plot the **market rate (Y-axis)** against the **job evaluation points (X-axis)** using **linear regression**:

$$
\text{Wage} = a + b \times (\text{Job Points})
$$

Where:
- $a$ = intercept (base wage for zero points — purely mathematical, rarely used in practice)
- $b$ = slope (₹ per evaluation point)

**Worked Example:**
Suppose the regression on a 25-job dataset yields:

$$
\text{Wage (₹)} = 8000 + 5.2 \times (\text{Job Points})
$$

For a job with **200 points**:
$$
\text{Wage} = 8000 + 5.2 \times 200 = 8000 + 1040 = \text{₹ 9,040}
$$

#### **Step 6: Establish Pay Grades**

Group jobs with **similar point ranges** into **pay grades**. A typical pay grade has a **Minimum, Midpoint, and Maximum**:

$$
\text{Midpoint} = \frac{\text{Minimum} + \text{Maximum}}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Range Spread} = \frac{\text{Max} - \text{Min}}{\text{Min}} \times 100
$$

**Standard industry benchmarks:**
- Lower grades (clerical): Range Spread = **25% to 35%**
- Middle grades (supervisory): Range Spread = **35% to 45%**
- Upper grades (managerial): Range Spread = **50% to 80%**

**Why the spread increases at higher grades?**
Because managerial performance variability is much wider, and to retain star performers without immediately promoting them.

#### **Step 7: Price Each Job**

Assign each job a pay grade based on its point total. The actual salary of an individual is then determined by:
- Their **performance rating**
- Their **Compa-Ratio target**
- Their **position in the salary range** (new hire, fully proficient, expert).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Framework

| Stage | Internal Equity Tool | External Competitiveness Tool | Individual Performance Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Job Analysis | Defines jobs | — | — |
| Job Evaluation | Compares jobs | — | — |
| Wage Survey | — | Anchors to market | — |
| Policy Formulation | Sets internal fairness | Sets market position | Sets merit philosophy |
| Pay Structure | Pay bands/grades | Range positions | Midpoint progression |
| Job Pricing | Maps jobs to bands | Compares to market | Tunes by performance |
| Administration | Annual increments | COLA, market revisions | Bonuses, incentives |

### 3.3 Derived Relationship — Compa-Ratio and Pay Position

The **Compa-Ratio** determines where an employee sits within the pay band:

$$
\text{CR} < 0.90 = \text{Underpaid (eligible for higher increment)}
$$

$$
0.90 \leq \text{CR} \leq 1.10 = \text{At-market (standard increment)}
$$

$$
\text{CR} > 1.10 = \text{Above-market (red-circle, freeze on increments)}
$$

This is a **critical HR governance formula** — it prevents pay drift, ensures budget control, and protects internal equity.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A pay grade has a Minimum of ₹30,000 and a Maximum of ₹54,000. An employee named Mr. Ramesh earns ₹42,000.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Midpoint} = \frac{30000 + 54000}{2} = \text{₹ 42,000}
$$

$$
\text{Range Spread} = \frac{54000 - 30000}{30000} \times 100 = \frac{24000}{30000} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

$$
\text{Compa-Ratio} = \frac{42000}{42000} = 1.00
$$

**Interpretation:** Mr. Ramesh is paid exactly at the midpoint of his grade — an **at-market, at-position** employee, eligible for standard annual increments.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always interpret every numerical answer with a one-line **managerial implication** to score the full 14 marks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**"Define wage determination. List any four internal factors influencing wage administration."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Wage determination is the systematic managerial process of fixing equitable wage rates for jobs by evaluating their relative worth through job analysis and job evaluation, and aligning them with external market pay data, legal standards, and the organization's ability to pay.

**Four Internal Factors (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Job Content** — complexity, difficulty, and skill demanded.
2. **Job Responsibility** — supervisory accountability and decision-making authority.
3. **Working Conditions** — hazardous or unpleasant environments warranting hazard pay.
4. **Organizational Ability to Pay** — determined by profitability and financial strength.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** Always write the definition in **2 to 3 crisp sentences** before listing factors. Examiners allocate 1 to 1.5 marks exclusively for the definition.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**"Differentiate between Job Evaluation and Wage Survey."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Dimension | Job Evaluation | Wage Survey |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Determines *internal* worth of jobs | Determines *external* market pay |
| **Focus** | Job content and compensable factors | Industry pay data and competitor rates |
| **Method** | Ranking, Point, Classification, Factor Comparison | Published surveys, direct benchmarking |
| **Output** | Job points / rank order | Median, P25, P50, P75 market rates |
| **Time Frame** | Internal, done by HR | External, done by survey agencies |

**Conclusion (for 0.5 mark):**
While job evaluation ensures **internal equity**, wage survey ensures **external competitiveness** — both are complementary pillars of wage determination.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 2 examinations typically provide **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions. Both questions below are designed as parallel alternatives.

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step process of wage determination** in detail. Discuss the importance of each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **external factors influencing wage and salary administration** in an organization, with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

### **(a) Step-by-Step Wage Determination Process (7 Marks)**

The wage determination process is a **systematic seven-stage workflow** that integrates internal job worth with external market realities.

**Stage 1 — Job Analysis (1 Mark):**
The process begins with a comprehensive job analysis involving interviews, questionnaires, and observation. It produces a **Job Description** (duties) and a **Job Specification** (human requirements). Without job analysis, all subsequent decisions would be based on guesswork.

**Stage 2 — Job Evaluation (1 Mark):**
Jobs are then compared using methods like **Ranking, Classification, Point Method, or Factor Comparison** to establish internal relativity. The most widely used method is the **Point Method** because it is objective, quantifiable, and legally defensible.

**Stage 3 — Wage and Salary Survey (1 Mark):**
The organization collects market data from competitors, industry publications, and consulting firms (e.g., Mercer, Aon Hewitt). The survey establishes the **prevailing market rate** (typically P25, P50, P75) for benchmark jobs, ensuring external competitiveness.

**Stage 4 — Wage Policy Formulation (1 Mark):**
Based on business strategy, the company decides whether to be a **wage leader** (pay above market — Google, Apple), **wage matcher** (pay at market — TCS, Infosys), or **wage lagger** (pay below market — common in cost-sensitive manufacturing). This decision drives the entire pay structure.

**Stage 5 — Designing the Wage Structure (1 Mark):**
Jobs are grouped into **pay grades** with defined minimum, midpoint, and maximum salaries. **Range Spread** and **Midpoint Progression** are computed to ensure orderly pay progression across grades.

**Stage 6 — Pricing Individual Jobs (1 Mark):**
Each evaluated job is mapped onto its appropriate pay grade, and an individual's actual salary is set based on performance, experience, and position within the band.

**Stage 7 — Administration and Review (1 Mark):**
The compensation system is maintained through **annual increments, performance bonuses, cost-of-living adjustments (COLA), and periodic wage surveys**. This stage ensures the pay structure remains relevant year after year.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** Always number your stages clearly and allocate roughly **0.5 to 1 mark per stage** with a one-line explanation.

---

### **(b) External Factors Influencing Wage Administration (7 Marks)**

External factors are conditions **outside the organization's direct control** that significantly shape wage levels.

**1. Demand and Supply of Labor (1 Mark):**
When skilled labor is scarce (e.g., AI engineers, data scientists), wages rise sharply. Conversely, when labor is abundant (e.g., unskilled textile workers), wages stagnate.

**2. Prevailing Market Rates (1 Mark):**
Competitor pay scales directly influence what an organization must pay to attract and retain talent. **Example:** If Infosys hikes salaries by 15%, TCS and Wipro must respond or risk attrition.

**3. Cost of Living (1 Mark):**
Measured by the **Consumer Price Index (CPI)**, cost of living triggers **Dearness Allowance (DA)** revisions in the public sector. When CPI rises, wages must rise to preserve **real wage** purchasing power.

$$
\text{Real Wage} = \frac{\text{Nominal Wage}}{\text{CPI}} \times 100
$$

**4. Industry Type and Competitive Intensity (1 Mark):**
Capital-intensive industries (oil, telecom) pay higher wages than labor-intensive industries (agriculture, handicrafts). High-skill industries (IT, pharma) pay premiums over low-skill industries.

**5. Geographic Location (1 Mark):**
Metropolitan salaries (Mumbai, Bangalore) are **30–50% higher** than tier-2 city salaries for the same job, due to higher cost of living and talent competition.

**6. Government Economic Policies (1 Mark):**
Fiscal stimulus, monetary tightening, and industrial policy influence corporate profitability and therefore the ability to pay wages.

**7. Labor Market Regulations and Union Activity (1 Mark):**
Strong trade unions (e.g., in banking and aviation) negotiate significantly higher wages through collective bargaining, often exceeding market rates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students only list the factors without explaining their **impact on wage levels**. To score full 7 marks, every factor must be tied back to how it **raises, lowers, or restructures** wages in the organization.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain any **four methods of job evaluation** with their merits and demerits. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** "Wage administration is influenced by both internal and external factors." Discuss with reference to **legal and trade union factors**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

### **(a) Four Methods of Job Evaluation (7 Marks)**

Job evaluation is the **backbone of internal equity**. Four classical methods are:

**1. Ranking Method (1.5 Marks):**
Jobs are ranked from highest to lowest based on overall judgment. **Merit:** Simple, fast, low-cost. **Demerit:** Highly subjective; no clear basis for ranking; difficult for large organizations.

**2. Job Classification Method (1.5 Marks):**
Jobs are grouped into predefined classes (e.g., Class I, II, III, IV) based on skill, responsibility, and difficulty. **Merit:** Easy to understand and administer. **Demerit:** Definitions of classes are subjective; difficult to slot edge-case jobs.

**3. Point Method (2 Marks):**
Jobs are scored on compensable factors (skill, effort, responsibility, working conditions), each with weighted degrees. **Merit:** Objective, quantitative, legally defensible, widely used. **Demerit:** Time-consuming; requires expert committee; rigid once established.

**Mathematical representation:**
$$
\text{Total Job Points} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Weight}_i \times \text{Degree}_i)
$$

**4. Factor Comparison Method (2 Marks):**
Benchmark jobs are priced and ranked on each factor separately; other jobs are evaluated by comparing to these benchmarks. **Merit:** More precise than ranking; customizable per factor. **Demerit:** Complex; difficult to explain to employees; limited adoption.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** When asked to "compare" methods, use a **table format** with at least three comparison dimensions: simplicity, objectivity, and suitability.

---

### **(b) Legal and Trade Union Factors (7 Marks)**

**Legal Factors (3.5 Marks):**

Several Indian laws govern wage administration:

1. **Minimum Wages Act, 1948** — fixes statutory minimum wages for scheduled employments, periodically revised.
2. **Payment of Wages Act, 1936** — ensures timely payment and limits unauthorized deductions.
3. **Payment of Bonus Act, 1965** — mandates minimum and maximum bonus based on profitability.
4. **Equal Remuneration Act, 1976** — prohibits gender-based wage discrimination.
5. **Factories Act, 1948** — prescribes overtime wages at **double the normal rate**.
6. **Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972** — mandates 15 days' wages per year of service after 5 years.

Non-compliance attracts penalties, prosecution, and reputational damage, making legal compliance a **non-negotiable constraint** on wage decisions.

**Trade Union Factors (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Union Strength** — strong unions in banking, aviation, and manufacturing negotiate **above-market** wages through industry-level settlements (e.g., **10th Bipartite Settlement** for bank employees).
2. **Bargaining Power** — unions with skilled negotiators secure richer benefits, not just higher pay.
3. **Collective Agreements** — wage structures are often legally bound by long-term agreements (3–5 years).
4. **Strike Threats and Industrial Action** — disruptive potential pressures management into wage concessions.
5. **Worker Participation** — unions increasingly influence **performance-based pay** and **productivity-linked bonuses**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Listing only Acts without explaining their **impact on wage decisions**. Each Act must be tied back to a specific wage implication (e.g., "Factories Act mandates double wages for overtime beyond 9 hours/week").
> - **Common mistake #2:** Confusing **trade union factors** with **employee associations**. Trade unions are legally registered entities with statutory recognition.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Failing to mention **collective bargaining** — examiners expect this term explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Wage Determination** = Job Analysis + Job Evaluation + Wage Survey + Policy + Structure + Pricing + Administration.
- ✅ **Salary Administration** = Continuous governance (increments, bonuses, COLA, revisions).
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Internal Equity, External Competitiveness, Individual Performance.
- ✅ **Internal Factors:** Job content, skill, responsibility, working conditions, ability to pay, performance, productivity.
- ✅ **External Factors:** Demand-supply, market rates, cost of living, industry, geography.
- ✅ **Economic Factors:** Inflation, CPI, GDP, government policy.
- ✅ **Legal Factors:** Minimum Wages Act 1948, Payment of Wages Act 1936, Bonus Act 1965, Equal Remuneration Act 1976, Factories Act 1948, Gratuity Act 1972.
- ✅ **Trade Union Factors:** Union strength, bargaining power, collective agreements, bipartite settlements.
- ✅ **Four Job Evaluation Methods:** Ranking, Classification, Point, Factor Comparison.
- ✅ **Compa-Ratio** = Actual Salary ÷ Midpoint (Ideal = 1.00).
- ✅ **Range Spread** = (Max − Min) ÷ Min × 100.
- ✅ **Real Wage** = Nominal Wage ÷ CPI × 100.
- ✅ **Halsey Plan** = 50% of time saved × hourly rate.
- ✅ **Rowan Plan** = (Time Taken / Time Allowed) × Time Saved × hourly rate.
- ✅ **Three Wage Policy Postures:** Leader (above P75), Match (at P50), Lagger (at P25).
- ✅ **Pay Grade Components:** Minimum, Midpoint, Maximum.
- ✅ **Midpoint Progression Benchmark:** 2.5% to 3.5% between grades.
- ✅ **Examiners love** the terms: *internal equity*, *external competitiveness*, *compensable factors*, *benchmark jobs*, *pay bands*, *collective bargaining*.
- ✅ **Always conclude** with managerial implications, examples, and the impact on organizational effectiveness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Wage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET751 - PERSONNEL MANAGEMENT AND INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Types of Wage # Types of Wage – KTU Premier Study Notes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wage** is a monetary compensation paid by an employer to an employee in exchange for the labour and services rendered, usually calculated on the basis of time spent, units produced, or tasks completed, in accordance with the contract of employment and applicable labour legislation. In KTU terminology aligned with the **Indian Factories Act, 1948** and **Payment of Wages Act, 1936**, wage represents the *remuneration payable in cash* and includes: - Basic pay - Dearness allowance (DA) - House rent allowance (HRA) in certain contexts - Overtime wages - Bonus and incentive payments - Any other remuneration expressed in **monetary terms** > [!NOTE] > **Key Statutory Metric:** According to the **Payment of Wages Act, 1936**, *wages must be paid in current coin or currency notes* or by cheque/bank credit. It does **not** include the value of any house accommodation, supply of light/water/medical attendance, or any other amenity. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you hire a friend to help you move furniture: - **Scenario A:** You pay them **₹100 per hour** for the time they spend working → this is a **Time Wage**. - **Scenario B:** You pay them **₹50 per box** they move, regardless of hours → this is a **Piece Wage**. - **Scenario C:** You guarantee a base hourly rate plus a bonus for every box beyond a target → this is a **Premium Bonus Plan** (like Halsey or Rowan). > [!TIP] > The fundamental difference between **wage systems** is the unit of measurement: *time, output, or a combination of both*. Each system shifts the **risk** of slow work between the employer and the employee differently. ### Standard Categorical Metrics - **Standard Hours per Day** = **8 hours** (Factories Act, 1948) - **Standard Working Days per Week** = **6 days / 48 hours** - **Overtime Rate** = **2 × ordinary rate** (Section 59, Factories Act) - **Minimum Wage Components** = Basic + DA + Special Allowance (Code on Wages, 2019) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Master Classification of Wage Systems Wage systems in industrial relations are classified into **three broad categories**: 1. **Time Wage System** (based on duration of work) 2. **Piece Wage System** (based on output produced) 3. **Combination / Incentive Wage Systems** (hybrid time-piece approaches) ### Sub-Classification Diagram ``` TYPES OF WAGE SYSTEMS ├── 1. Time Wage System │ ├── Single Time Rate │ └── Graded Time Rate ├── 2. Piece Wage System │ ├── Straight Piece Rate │ └── Differential Piece Rate │ ├── Taylor's Differential Piece Rate │ └── Merrick's Differential Piece Rate └── 3. Combination / Premium Bonus Plans ├── Halsey Plan ├── Rowan Plan ├── Gantt's Task and Bonus Plan ├── Bedaux Plan ├── Emerson's Efficiency Plan └── Profit Sharing Scheme ``` ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **exam-ready**. Master these — they are the most frequently tested numerical concepts under the "Types of Wage" topic. | **Plan Name** | **Earnings Formula** | **Time Saved (T_S)** | **Bonus Trigger** | |---|---|---|---| | **Time Wage** | $E = R \times H$ | None | None | | **Straight Piece Rate** | $E = P \times N$ | None | None | | **Halsey Plan** | $E = R \times T + (R \times T_S) \times \frac{50}{100}$ | $T_S = S - A$ | When $A < S$ | | **Rowan Plan** | $E = R \times A + (R \times S) \times \frac{T_S}{S}$ | $T_S = S - A$ | When $A < S$ | | **Taylor's Differential** | $E_{low} = 0.80 \times P \times N$
$E_{high} = 1.20 \times P \times N$ | None | Efficiency threshold | | **Merrick's Differential** | $E = P \times N \times k$
(k = 1.0, 1.10, 1.20, 1.30) | None | Efficiency tier | | **Gantt's Task-Bonus** | Bonus = 33% of Basic Pay when standard met | Task Completion | Standard met | | **Emerson's Plan** | Bonus = 20% × Hourly Rate (when efficiency ≥ 66.7%) | None | Efficiency ≥ 67% | | **Bedaux Plan** | $E = (B \times H) + (B \times T_S) \times 0.75$ | $T_S = S - A$ | 75% of savings | | **Profit Sharing** | $E = W + (P_p - T_c) \times k$ | None | Company profit | > **Legend:** > - $R$ = Hourly/Daily Rate > - $H$ = Hours worked > - $P$ = Piece rate per unit > - $N$ = Number of units produced > - $S$ = Standard time allowed > - $A$ = Actual time taken > - $T_S$ = Time saved > - $B$ = Bedaux standard hours per unit > - $P_p$ = Total profit, $T_c$ = Target profit, $k$ = Sharing ratio > - $W$ = Regular wages ### Critical Boundary Conditions - **Halsey:** Bonus is fixed at **50% of time saved** (independent of efficiency level) - **Rowan:** Bonus is proportional — the *higher the efficiency*, the *lower the percentage share* to worker (and vice versa) - **Taylor:** Below standard → worker gets **80%** of piece rate; at/above standard → worker gets **120%** - **Merrick:** Three tiers of efficiency → progressive bonus structure - **Emerson:** Below **66.67%** efficiency → no bonus; above → 20% bonus + accelerated premium above 100% --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis ### Worked Example 1: Halsey Plan > **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – June 2023]:** > A worker's standard hourly rate is **₹40**. Standard time allowed for a job is **20 hours**, but the worker completes it in **16 hours**. Calculate total earnings under the **Halsey Premium Plan**. **Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):** Given: - Hourly Rate ($R$) = ₹40 - Standard Time ($S$) = 20 hours - Actual Time ($A$) = 16 hours **Step 1: Calculate time saved** $$T_S = S - A = 20 - 16 = 4 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 2: Calculate wages for actual time worked** $$\text{Wages} = R \times A = 40 \times 16 = ₹640$$ **Step 3: Calculate bonus under Halsey (50% of time saved)** $$\text{Bonus} = R \times T_S \times \frac{50}{100}$$ $$\text{Bonus} = 40 \times 4 \times 0.5 = ₹80$$ **Step 4: Total Earnings** $$\text{Total Earnings (Halsey)} = R \times A + (R \times T_S) \times \frac{50}{100}$$ $$\text{Total Earnings (Halsey)} = 640 + 80 = ₹720$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Efficiency Check:** $\text{Efficiency} = \frac{S}{A} \times 100 = \frac{20}{16} \times 100 = 125\%$ --- ### Worked Example 2: Rowan Plan > **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]:** > Same worker as above. Standard rate = ₹40/hr, Standard time = 20 hrs, Actual time = 16 hrs. Calculate earnings under the **Rowan Premium Plan**. **Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):** **Step 1: Time saved** $$T_S = 20 - 16 = 4 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 2: Wages for actual time** $$\text{Wages} = 40 \times 16 = ₹640$$ **Step 3: Bonus under Rowan = proportional to time saved** $$\text{Bonus} = R \times S \times \frac{T_S}{S} = R \times T_S$$ Wait — reapply the correct formula: $$\text{Bonus} = R \times S \times \frac{(S - A)}{S} = R \times (S - A)$$ But the **Rowan bonus** is: $$\text{Bonus} = (R \times S) \times \frac{T_S}{S} = (R \times S) \times \frac{S - A}{S}$$ $$\text{Bonus} = (40 \times 20) \times \frac{4}{20} = 800 \times 0.2 = ₹160$$ **Step 4: Total Earnings** $$\text{Total Earnings (Rowan)} = R \times A + (R \times S) \times \frac{(S - A)}{S}$$ $$\text{Total Earnings (Rowan)} = 640 + 160 = ₹800$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Comparative Insight:** At 125% efficiency, Rowan pays ₹800 vs Halsey's ₹720. Rowan is more attractive to **highly efficient workers**. --- ### Worked Example 3: Taylor's Differential Piece Rate > **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – June 2022]:** > Standard output = **40 units/day**. Piece rate = **₹5/unit**. Worker A produced **30 units** and Worker B produced **50 units** in a day. Compute earnings under **Taylor's Differential Piece Rate System**. **Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):** **Step 1: Compute efficiency of each worker** $$\text{Efficiency}_A = \frac{30}{40} \times 100 = 75\%$$ $$\text{Efficiency}_B = \frac{50}{40} \times 100 = 125\%$$ **Step 2: Apply Taylor's rates** - Below standard → **80% of piece rate** - At/above standard → **120% of piece rate** **Worker A (Below Standard):** $$\text{Earnings}_A = 0.80 \times P \times N = 0.80 \times 5 \times 30 = ₹120$$ **Worker B (Above Standard):** $$\text{Earnings}_B = 1.20 \times P \times N = 1.20 \times 5 \times 50 = ₹300$$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Note:** In Taylor's plan, worker B earns ₹300 for producing 50 units — substantially more than Worker A's ₹120 for only 30 units. This wide gap creates **strong incentive**. --- ### Worked Example 4: Merrick's Differential Piece Rate > **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – Dec 2021]:** > Standard output = **30 units/day**. Piece rate = **₹6/unit**. Three workers produced 20, 35, and 45 units respectively. Calculate wages using the **Merrick Plan**. **Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):** | **Worker** | **Output** | **Efficiency** | **Tier** | **Multiplier** | **Earnings** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | X | 20 | 66.67% | Below 83% | 1.00× | $6 \times 20 \times 1.00 = ₹120$ | | Y | 35 | 116.67% | 100%–125% | 1.20× | $6 \times 35 \times 1.20 = ₹252$ | | Z | 45 | 150% | Above 125% | 1.30× | $6 \times 45 \times 1.30 = ₹351$ | $$\text{Earnings}_X = ₹120, \quad \text{Earnings}_Y = ₹252, \quad \text{Earnings}_Z = ₹351$$ --- ### Worked Example 5: Gantt's Task and Bonus Plan > **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – May 2022]:** > Daily wage = ₹400. Standard output = 100 units. Worker completes 120 units. Calculate earnings under **Gantt's Task and Bonus Plan**. **Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):** **Step 1:** Determine hourly rate (assuming 8-hour day) $$R = \frac{400}{8} = ₹50/\text{hr}$$ **Step 2:** If worker meets standard (≥100 units), bonus = **33⅓% of basic pay** $$\text{Bonus} = \frac{1}{3} \times 400 = ₹133.33$$ **Step 3:** Total Earnings $$\text{Total Earnings (Gantt)} = 400 + 133.33 = ₹533.33$$ > [!NOTE] > **Gantt's Distinctive Feature:** If the worker does **not** meet standard but completes the task within standard *time*, he still receives a bonus. If he fails both, he gets only **80%** of basic pay. --- ### Worked Example 6: Emerson's Efficiency Plan > **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]:** > Standard time = 10 hours. Hourly rate = ₹60. Actual time taken = 8 hours. Find the total earnings under **Emerson's Plan**. **Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):** **Step 1: Efficiency** $$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{10}{8} \times 100 = 125\%$$ **Step 2: Guaranteed wages for actual time** $$W = 60 \times 8 = ₹480$$ **Step 3: Bonus = 20% of hourly rate × hours worked (since efficiency ≥ 66.67%)** $$\text{Bonus} = 0.20 \times 60 \times 8 = ₹96$$ **Step 4: Total Earnings** $$\text{Total Earnings (Emerson)} = 480 + 96 = ₹576$$ --- ### Worked Example 7: Bedaux Plan > **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – June 2024]:** > Standard hours per unit (B) = 2. Piece rate equivalent = ₹50/hr. Worker produced 10 units in **15 hours**. Calculate earnings under **Bedaux Plan**. **Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):** **Step 1: Standard hours allowed** $$S = B \times N = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 2: Time saved** $$T_S = S - A = 20 - 15 = 5 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 3: Base earnings** $$\text{Base} = B \times H = 50 \times 15 = ₹750$$ **Step 4: Bonus = 75% of time saved × rate** $$\text{Bonus} = 0.75 \times 50 \times 5 = ₹187.50$$ **Step 5: Total Earnings** $$\text{Total Earnings (Bedaux)} = 750 + 187.50 = ₹937.50$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Master Wage System Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Select Wage System] --> B{Quality Critical?} B -- Yes --> C[Time Wage System] B -- No --> D{Output Quantifiable?} D -- No --> C D -- Yes --> E{Workers are Skilled?} E -- No --> F[Piece Wage System] E -- Yes --> G{Want Strong Incentive?} G -- No --> H[Halsey or Rowan Plan] G -- Yes --> I{Differential Output Rewarded?} I -- Yes --> J[Taylor or Merrick Plan] I -- No --> K[Gantt or Emerson Plan] C --> L[End: Compensation Decided] F --> L H --> L J --> L K --> L ``` ### Diagram 2: Halsey vs Rowan Comparison Architecture ```mermaid graph LR subgraph H[HALSEY PLAN] H1[Time Saved: S - A] --> H2[Bonus = 50% of Time Saved] H2 --> H3[Constant 50% Share] end subgraph R[ROWAN PLAN] R1[Time Saved: S - A] --> R2[Bonus proportional to S-A over S] R2 --> R3[Variable Share: T_S divided by S] end H3 --> X[Total Wage] R3 --> X ``` ### Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Premium Bonus Plans ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT[INPUT STAGE] I1[Standard Time S] I2[Actual Time A] I3[Hourly Rate R] end subgraph PROCESS[PROCESSING ENGINE] P1[Calculate Time Saved T_S = S - A] P2[Compute Efficiency = S divided by A times 100] P3[Determine Bonus per Plan Logic] P4[Compute Base Pay = R times A] end subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT STAGE] O1[Halsey: Base + 50 percent of T_S at R] O2[Rowan: Base + R times S times T_S over S] O3[Taylor: 80 percent or 120 percent of Piece Rate] O4[Gantt: Base + 33.3 percent if Standard Met] O5[Bedaux: Base + 75 percent of T_S at Rate] end I1 --> P1 I2 --> P1 I1 --> P2 I2 --> P2 I3 --> P4 P1 --> P3 P3 --> O1 P3 --> O2 P2 --> O3 P2 --> O4 P1 --> O5 P4 --> O1 P4 --> O2 P4 --> O4 P4 --> O5 ``` ### Diagram 4: Taylor's Differential Piece Rate Tiers ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Worker Output] --> B{Efficiency vs Standard} B -- Below Standard: Less than 100% --> C[Pay 80 percent of Piece Rate] B -- At or Above Standard: 100% or more --> D[Pay 120 percent of Piece Rate] C --> E[Lower Incentive Zone] D --> F[High Incentive Zone] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Define Time Wage System. List any two of its merits.** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** Time Wage System is a method of wage payment in which workers are paid a fixed rate per unit of time (hour, day, week, or month) regardless of the quantum of work completed during that period. **Two Merits:** 1. **Quality Assurance:** Since payment is independent of output, workers tend to focus on **quality** rather than quantity — especially useful in precision industries. 2. **Simple Computation:** The calculation involves only **time worked × rate**, making it straightforward to administer with minimal clerical effort. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** > **Distinguish between Halsey Plan and Rowan Plan.** **Model Answer:** | **Basis** | **Halsey Plan** | **Rowan Plan** | |---|---|---| | Bonus Formula | 50% of time saved × rate | Proportional to time saved / standard time | | Bonus % | Fixed at 50% | Variable with efficiency | | Worker Benefit | More attractive to **average** workers | More attractive to **highly efficient** workers | | Risk Sharing | 50:50 between worker and employer | Varies by efficiency level | --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, students must answer **ONE full question** from the module internal choice. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **Taylor's Differential Piece Rate System** with its merits and limitations. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A worker completes a job in **15 hours** against a standard of **20 hours**. His hourly rate is **₹50**. Compute his total earnings under: - (i) Halsey Plan - (ii) Rowan Plan - (iii) Bedaux Plan **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Taylor's Differential Piece Rate System:** Introduced by **F.W. Taylor** (Father of Scientific Management), this system establishes **two distinct piece rates**: - **Low Rate:** 80% of normal piece rate for workers producing **below standard** - **High Rate:** 120% of normal piece rate for workers producing **at or above standard** **Merits:** 1. Strong incentive for higher output 2. Discourages slow workers via steep penalty 3. Simple to understand and apply **Limitations:** 1. Creates worker dissatisfaction at the "penalty zone" 2. Quality may suffer due to speed focus 3. Difficult to set accurate standard rates --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: $S = 20$ hrs, $A = 15$ hrs, $R = ₹50$, $T_S = 20 - 15 = 5$ hrs **(i) Halsey Plan:** $$\text{Earnings} = R \times A + (R \times T_S) \times \frac{50}{100}$$ $$= 50 \times 15 + (50 \times 5) \times 0.5 = 750 + 125 = ₹875$$ **(ii) Rowan Plan:** $$\text{Earnings} = R \times A + (R \times S) \times \frac{(S - A)}{S}$$ $$= 750 + (50 \times 20) \times \frac{5}{20} = 750 + 250 = ₹1000$$ **(iii) Bedaux Plan (assuming B = R for simplicity):** $$\text{Earnings} = R \times A + (R \times T_S) \times 0.75$$ $$= 750 + (50 \times 5) \times 0.75 = 750 + 187.50 = ₹937.50$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Describe the **Merrick's Differential Piece Rate System**. Compare it with Taylor's plan. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The standard output per day for a worker is **100 units**. The piece rate is **₹4 per unit**. Workers A, B, and C produced **70, 110, and 130** units respectively. Calculate their wages under: - (i) Straight Piece Rate - (ii) Merrick's Plan **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Merrick's Differential Piece Rate System:** Introduced by **V.E. Merrick**, this is a **modified and less harsh version** of Taylor's plan with **three tiers**: | **Efficiency Range** | **Multiplier** | |---|---| | Up to 83.33% | 1.00 × piece rate | | 83.34% – 100% | 1.10 × piece rate | | 100% – 125% | 1.20 × piece rate | | Above 125% | 1.30 × piece rate | **Comparison with Taylor:** | **Basis** | **Taylor** | **Merrick** | |---|---|---| | Penalty Zone | Severe (80%) | Gradual (100%) | | Tiers | 2 tiers | 4 tiers | | Worker Acceptance | Lower | Higher | | Incentive Strength | Very High | Moderate-High | --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: Standard = 100 units, Piece Rate ($P$) = ₹4 **(i) Straight Piece Rate:** | Worker | Output | Wages = $P \times N$ | |---|---|---| | A | 70 | $4 \times 70 = ₹280$ | | B | 110 | $4 \times 110 = ₹440$ | | C | 130 | $4 \times 130 = ₹520$ | **(ii) Merrick's Plan:** | Worker | Output | Efficiency | Tier | Multiplier | Wages | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | 70 | 70% | <83.33% | 1.00× | $4 \times 70 \times 1.00 = ₹280$ | | B | 110 | 110% | 100%–125% | 1.20× | $4 \times 110 \times 1.20 = ₹528$ | | C | 130 | 130% | >125% | 1.30× | $4 \times 130 \times 1.30 = ₹676$ | --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Halsey & Rowan formulas** — Rowan bonus uses $R \times S \times \frac{T_S}{S}$, **not** $R \times T_S$. > 2. **Forgetting efficiency** in Taylor/Merrick plans — always compute efficiency first before selecting the tier. > 3. **Mixing time units** — convert all time to a uniform unit (hours) before applying formulas. > 4. **Gantt's Bonus ≠ 33.33%** of base pay when standard NOT met — only the **80% penalty clause** applies. > 5. **Profit Sharing ≠ Bonus** — Profit sharing is based on company profit, not individual time saved. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - **Wage** = monetary compensation for labour rendered, paid as per contract and labour laws. - **Time Wage** = paid per unit of time; suitable for **quality-critical** work. - **Piece Wage** = paid per unit produced; suitable for **quantifiable output**. - **Halsey Plan** = Fixed **50% bonus** on time saved → rewards average workers. - **Rowan Plan** = Proportional bonus → rewards highly efficient workers more. - **Taylor's Plan** = 80% (below standard) vs 120% (above standard) → strong but harsh. - **Merrick's Plan** = 4-tier progressive system → gentler than Taylor. - **Gantt's Plan** = 33⅓% bonus when standard met → emphasizes task completion. - **Emerson's Plan** = 20% bonus above 66.67% efficiency → includes learning curve logic. - **Bedaux Plan** = 75% of time saved to worker, 25% to employer → "Standard Hour Plan." - **Profit Sharing** = share of profit distributed to workers → aligns employee interest with company. - **Examiners' Hot Topics:** Halsey vs Rowan comparison, Taylor's plan, numerical computation of total earnings. - **Formula Recap:** - $E_{Halsey} = R \times A + 0.5 \times R \times (S - A)$ - $E_{Rowan} = R \times A + R \times S \times \frac{(S-A)}{S}$ - $E_{Bedaux} = R \times A + 0.75 \times R \times (S - A)$ - $E_{Taylor\,Low} = 0.80 \times P \times N$; $E_{Taylor\,High} = 1.20 \times P \times N$ - $E_{Merrick} = P \times N \times k$ where $k \in \{1.00, 1.10, 1.20, 1.30\}$

topics.title: Minimum Wage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET751 - PERSONNEL MANAGEMENT AND INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minimum Wage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Minimum Wage** is defined as the wage which must provide not only the bare subsistence of life but also the preservation of the efficiency of the worker. For this purpose, the minimum wage must also provide for some measure of education, medical requirements, and amenities. It is the lowest wage legally permissible that an employer is bound to pay to a worker in a scheduled employment under the **Minimum Wages Act, 1948**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Tripartite Committee, 1948):** *"A minimum wage must be sufficient to provide for the bare subsistence of the worker and his family, even in the lowest paid employments, without taking into account any extraneous circumstances."*

The concept is rooted in three foundational components:
- **Bare Subsistence** — Basic requirements of life (food, clothing, shelter)
- **Efficiency Preservation** — Maintenance of health and working capacity
- **Some Measure of Education & Amenities** — To prevent the worker from sinking into the lowest level of degradation

### 1.2 Hierarchical Wage Structure (Wage Pyramid)

The Indian wage system follows a **three-tier wage structure** as recommended by the **15th Indian Labour Conference (1957)** and the **Committee on Fair Wages (1948)**:

| Tier | Wage Type | Definition |
|------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | **Minimum Wage** | Lowest wage necessary to sustain life and preserve efficiency |
| 2 | **Fair Wage** | Wage between minimum and living, determined by industry's capacity to pay |
| 3 | **Living Wage** | Wage that maintains a healthy, decent standard of living |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Survival Threshold"**
>
> Imagine a **fuel gauge** in a car. The Minimum Wage is the **"E" (Empty) mark** on the gauge — it is the bare threshold below which the vehicle cannot run. The **Fair Wage** is the fuel level that allows comfortable commuting, while the **Living Wage** is a full tank that lets you undertake long journeys with family and amenities.
>
> In HR terms: if you pay below the Minimum Wage, the worker cannot even refuel his basic existence; if you pay only at Minimum, the worker survives but cannot grow; Fair Wage keeps the worker content; Living Wage enables prosperity.

### 1.4 Minimum Wages Act, 1948 — Key Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Legal Provision:** The Act aims to prevent exploitation of workers by ensuring a statutory minimum wage in **scheduled employments** (both agriculture and non-agriculture) where labour is unorganized and workers are vulnerable to underpayment.

**Key Features:**
- Applies to scheduled employments listed in the schedule
- Both **time-rated** and **piece-rated** workers are covered
- Wage rates are revised at intervals not exceeding **5 years**
- **Inspector** appointed to enforce the Act
- Penalty provisions: imprisonment up to **6 months** and/or fine up to **₹500** for first offence

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theories of Wages (Foundational Knowledge)

The determination of minimum wage cannot be understood in isolation — it is rooted in classical economic theories of wages:

#### 2.1.1 Subsistence Theory of Wages (David Ricardo)
- **Core Idea:** Wages tend to settle at the **subsistence level** (the minimum required to keep the worker and family alive).
- **Mechanism:** When wages rise above subsistence → population grows → labour supply increases → wages fall back to subsistence.
- **Critique:** Ignores human dignity, productivity, and demand-side factors.

#### 2.1.2 Wage Fund Theory (John Stuart Mill)
- **Core Idea:** Wages depend on the ratio of **total wage fund** to the **number of workers**.
- **Formula:** $W = \dfrac{F}{N}$
- Where: $W$ = Wage per worker, $F$ = Total wage fund, $N$ = Number of workers
- **Critique:** Wage fund is not fixed; modern economics shows wages come from current production.

#### 2.1.3 Marginal Productivity Theory (J.B. Clark)
- **Core Idea:** A worker is paid the value of the **marginal product** he/she produces.
- **Mechanism:** Under perfect competition, $Wage = Marginal\ Revenue\ Product\ of\ Labour$
- **Critique:** Ignores bargaining power, monopsony, and minimum wage laws.

#### 2.1.4 Supply and Demand Theory
- **Core Idea:** Wages are determined by the intersection of labour supply and labour demand.
- **Mechanism:** Wage rates rise when demand exceeds supply; fall when supply exceeds demand.

#### 2.1.5 Behavioural Theory
- **Core Idea:** Wages are not merely economic; they reflect **social, psychological, and behavioural factors** like fairness, equity, and motivation (Maslow, Herzberg).

### 2.2 Methods of Fixing Minimum Wages

| Method | Description | Applicability |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| **Committee Method** | Tripartite committees (employer, employee, government) fix wages | Most common in India |
| **Collective Bargaining** | Direct negotiation between unions and employers | Organized sectors |
| **Wage Board** | Statutory body fixes wages for specific industries | Newspapers, sugar, cement, etc. |
| **Legislation** | Statutory enactment by government | Minimum Wages Act, 1948 |
| **Job Evaluation** | Systematic assessment of job worth to determine wages | Modern HRM practice |
| **Industrial Jurisprudence** | Wage determination through judicial decisions | National Wage Tribunal cases |

### 2.3 Factors Affecting Minimum Wage Fixation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Area:** The Tripartite Committee and the **Indian Labour Conference (15th Session, 1957)** prescribed **norms** for determining minimum wage. These are **mandatory to remember** for KTU exams.

**Six Norms for Minimum Wage (per month, for a single male worker, in 1957 prices):**

1. **Basic food** equivalent to **3,000 calories per day** (rice/wheat-based diet)
2. **Clothing** consumption at **72 yards per year** (15 metres)
3. **Rent** — a portion equivalent to **5% of total wage**
4. **Fuel, lighting, and other miscellaneous** expenditure
5. **Education, medical, and recreation** allowance
6. **Provision for old age, marriage, and contingencies** — equivalent to **15% of the total wage**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Formula Box for Minimum Wage Calculation**

$$
\text{Minimum Wage} = \text{Food Cost} + \text{Clothing Cost} + \text{Housing Cost} + \text{Fuel \& Lighting} + \text{Miscellaneous} + \text{Children Education} + \text{Medical} + \text{Recreation} + \text{Contingency Reserve}
$$

| Component | Norm (Monthly, 1957) | Adjustment Formula |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Food (3,000 cal/day) | ₹25 per month | $C_{food} = \text{Quantity} \times \text{Price Index}$ |
| Clothing (72 yds/yr) | ₹3.5 per month | $C_{cloth} = \dfrac{72}{12} \times \text{Price Index}$ |
| Housing (5% of wage) | Variable | $C_{hous} = 0.05 \times W$ |
| Fuel & Lighting | ₹2 per month | $C_{fuel} = \text{Quantity} \times \text{Price Index}$ |
| Misc. (sundries) | ₹3 per month | $C_{misc} = 3 \times CPI$ |
| Education/Medical/Recreation | ₹2.5 per month | $C_{emr} = 2.5 \times CPI$ |
| Contingency (15% of wage) | Variable | $C_{cont} = 0.15 \times W$ |
| **Total (1957 base)** | **₹36 per month** | Revised using CPI |

**Adjusted Minimum Wage Formula (Modern Application):**

$$
W_{min,current} = W_{min,base} \times \dfrac{CPI_{current}}{CPI_{base}}
$$

Where $CPI$ is the **Consumer Price Index** (Index Number of Industrial Workers).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering/Industry Utility

In modern HRM and industrial engineering:
- **Wage design** is integral to **work-study, time-study, and job evaluation** in manufacturing
- **Total Quality Management (TQM)** — fair wages are essential for worker engagement
- **Industry 4.0 / Factory automation** — even in automated industries, minimum wage policies affect the human workforce for maintenance, supervision, and quality control
- **ILO Convention No. 131 (1970)** — provides international framework for minimum wage fixing

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation: Minimum Wage Calculation (Numerical Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Standard KTU-style):**
> Calculate the minimum wage of a single male industrial worker based on the norms prescribed by the 15th Indian Labour Conference (1957), given the following data:
> - Rice price: ₹40 per kg (current)
> - Rice consumption: 90 grams per day (per 3,000 calories requirement)
> - Clothing: 72 yards per year at ₹100 per yard (current)
> - Rent: 5% of total wage
> - Fuel & lighting: ₹300 per month
> - Miscellaneous (sundries): ₹500 per month
> - Education/medical/recreation: ₹400 per month
> - Contingency reserve: 15% of total wage
> - Base 1957 wage: ₹36/month with base CPI = 100; current CPI = 350

---

#### **STEP 1: Calculate Food Cost**

Rice consumption per month (assuming 30 days):

$$
\text{Monthly Rice} = 90 \text{ grams/day} \times 30 \text{ days} = 2{,}700 \text{ grams} = 2.7 \text{ kg}
$$

Cost of rice per month:

$$
C_{food} = 2.7 \text{ kg} \times ₹40/\text{kg} = ₹108
$$

#### **STEP 2: Calculate Clothing Cost**

Cloth per month:

$$
\text{Monthly Cloth} = \dfrac{72 \text{ yards}}{12 \text{ months}} = 6 \text{ yards}
$$

Cost of clothing per month:

$$
C_{cloth} = 6 \text{ yards} \times ₹100/\text{yard} = ₹600
$$

#### **STEP 3: Housing Cost (5% of W)**

$$
C_{hous} = 0.05 \times W
$$

(where $W$ is the total monthly wage — to be determined)

#### **STEP 4: Fuel & Lighting**

$$
C_{fuel} = ₹300
$$

#### **STEP 5: Miscellaneous**

$$
C_{misc} = ₹500
$$

#### **STEP 6: Education/Medical/Recreation**

$$
C_{emr} = ₹400
$$

#### **STEP 7: Contingency Reserve (15% of W)**

$$
C_{cont} = 0.15 \times W
$$

#### **STEP 8: Apply the Equation**

$$
W = C_{food} + C_{cloth} + C_{hous} + C_{fuel} + C_{misc} + C_{emr} + C_{cont}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
W = 108 + 600 + 0.05W + 300 + 500 + 400 + 0.15W
$$

$$
W = 108 + 600 + 300 + 500 + 400 + (0.05W + 0.15W)
$$

$$
W = 1{,}908 + 0.20W
$$

$$
W - 0.20W = 1{,}908
$$

$$
0.80W = 1{,}908
$$

$$
W = \dfrac{1{,}908}{0.80} = ₹2{,}385
$$

#### **STEP 9: Verify Housing and Contingency**

- Housing: $0.05 \times 2{,}385 = ₹119.25$
- Contingency: $0.15 \times 2{,}385 = ₹357.75$
- Sum of variable components: $119.25 + 357.75 = ₹477$ ✓ (matches $0.20W$)

#### **STEP 10: Apply CPI Adjustment (Cross-Verification)**

$$
W_{current} = W_{base} \times \dfrac{CPI_{current}}{CPI_{base}}
$$

$$
W_{current} = 36 \times \dfrac{350}{100} = 36 \times 3.5 = ₹126
$$

(1957 base is purely indicative; modern minimum wages are much higher in actual practice due to higher standards of living. The detailed component method (Step 1–9) is the **more accurate modern approach**.)

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Wage Frameworks

| Dimension | Minimum Wage | Fair Wage | Living Wage |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Definition** | Bare subsistence + efficiency preservation | Wage above minimum, below living, based on industry capacity | Wage for healthy, decent living standard |
| **Set By** | Government (statute/committee) | Tripartite settlement / judicial awards | Determined by ILO principles & national policy |
| **Varies With** | Cost of living | Capacity of industry to pay | Local economic conditions |
| **Applies To** | Unorganized & scheduled sectors | Organized, collective bargaining sectors | Aspirational / developmental goal |
| **Legal Status** | Statutory / mandatory | Contractual / not always statutory | Directive principle, not enforceable |
| **Lower Bound** | Subsistence level (₹36 in 1957 norms) | Above minimum | Above fair wage |
| **Upper Bound** | Below fair wage | Below living wage | No fixed upper bound |

### 3.3 Wage Determination Flow

$$
\text{Industry Capacity} \;\times\; \text{Cost of Living} \;\times\; \text{Productivity} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Fair Wage}
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Living (Minimum Standard)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Minimum Wage}
$$

$$
\text{Needs-Based Income (Decent Living)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Living Wage}
$$

The **Fair Wage** is the geometric mean between the minimum and living wage in economic theory, though in practice it is determined by the ability of the industry to pay.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Minimum Wage. What are the components considered while fixing it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Minimum wage is the lowest wage that an employer is legally required to pay to a worker to ensure bare subsistence and preservation of efficiency. As per the Minimum Wages Act, 1948, it applies to scheduled employments and is fixed by the government through committees or notifications.

**Components (1 mark):** The components are: (i) Food, (ii) Clothing, (iii) Housing, (iv) Fuel & lighting, (v) Education/medical/recreation, and (vi) Provision for contingencies (~15% of wage).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Distinguish between Minimum Wage and Living Wage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Minimum Wage | Living Wage |
|--------|--------------|-------------|
| **Level** | Subsistence level (lower) | Healthy, decent standard of living (higher) |
| **Determination** | Fixed by statute/committee | Aspirational, based on economic development |
| **Coverage** | Statutory (Minimum Wages Act, 1948) | Directive principle, not strictly enforceable |
| **Revision** | Every 5 years | No fixed revision cycle |
| **Focus** | Bare survival + efficiency | Family welfare, education, social security |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three-tier wage structure** (Minimum, Fair, and Living Wage) as recommended by the **Committee on Fair Wages (1948)**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **theories of wages** — Subsistence Theory, Wage Fund Theory, and Marginal Productivity Theory. How do they help in understanding minimum wage fixation? (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

The **Committee on Fair Wages (1948)**, also known as the **B.N. Adarkar Committee**, was a tripartite body set up by the Government of India to recommend a framework for wage determination in India. The committee proposed a **three-tier wage structure**:

**1. Minimum Wage (Bottom Tier):** This is the wage that must provide the worker with bare subsistence and preserve his efficiency. The committee laid down the **six norms** (food = 3,000 calories/day, clothing = 72 yards/year, housing, fuel, education/medical, contingency = 15% of wage). The minimum wage is fixed by the government under the **Minimum Wages Act, 1948** and is statutorily enforceable.

**2. Fair Wage (Middle Tier):** Fair wage is the wage which is **above the minimum wage** but **below the living wage**. It is determined by the **capacity of the industry to pay** and is influenced by factors like productivity, market conditions, and the prevailing wage rate. Fair wage is usually fixed through **collective bargaining, wage boards, or industrial tribunals**.

**3. Living Wage (Top Tier):** Living wage aims to provide the worker with a standard of living suitable for the full development of his personality. It goes beyond mere subsistence and includes food, clothing, housing, education, healthcare, and a measure of insurance against old age, accidents, and unemployment. Living wage is the **aspirational wage** and depends on the overall economic development of the country.

**Relationship:** The three are interconnected — minimum wage is the **floor**, living wage is the **ceiling**, and fair wage fluctuates in between based on industry-specific factors.

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**1. Subsistence Theory (David Ricardo, 1817):** This theory states that wages tend to settle at the **subsistence level** — the minimum required to keep the worker and his family alive. The mechanism is the "iron law of wages": when wages rise above subsistence, population increases, leading to more labour supply, which pushes wages back down. The **relevance to minimum wage** is that it provides the philosophical basis for ensuring wages do not fall below subsistence — a core principle of the Minimum Wages Act.

**2. Wage Fund Theory (John Stuart Mill, 1869):** According to this theory, $W = F/N$, where $W$ is the wage rate, $F$ is the total wage fund, and $N$ is the number of workers. The wage fund is assumed to be **fixed** in the short run. The **relevance to minimum wage** is limited, as modern economics shows the wage fund is not fixed, but it historically influenced early wage policy thinking.

**3. Marginal Productivity Theory (J.B. Clark, 1899):** This theory states that a worker is paid the value of his **marginal contribution** to production. Under perfect competition, $W = MRP_L$ (Marginal Revenue Product of Labour). The **relevance to minimum wage** is that it provides the demand-side justification — wages cannot be set below the marginal product if labour is to be employed efficiently.

**Synthesis:** These theories together explain the **economic rationale** behind minimum wage fixation: the **floor** is set by subsistence needs (Subsistence Theory), the **ceiling** by productivity (Marginal Productivity Theory), and the **funds available** by the wage fund (Wage Fund Theory).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **methods of fixing minimum wages** in India. Which method is most prevalent and why? (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **features, objectives, and shortcomings** of the **Minimum Wages Act, 1948**. (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

The methods of fixing minimum wages in India are:

**1. Committee Method (Tripartite Committee):** A committee comprising representatives of workers, employers, and the government fixes the minimum wage based on prescribed norms. **Most prevalent in India** for unorganized sectors.

**2. Collective Bargaining:** Trade unions negotiate with employers to fix wages, common in organized sectors like banking, insurance, and manufacturing.

**3. Wage Boards:** Statutory bodies constituted for specific industries (e.g., newspaper industry, sugar industry, cement industry) to recommend wage structures.

**4. Legislation:** Direct statutory enactment by the government, e.g., the Minimum Wages Act, 1948 and the Payment of Wages Act, 1936.

**5. Industrial Jurisprudence:** Wage determination through judicial decisions of Industrial Tribunals, Labour Courts, and the National Industrial Tribunal.

**6. Job Evaluation:** Scientific assessment of job worth based on skills, effort, responsibility, and working conditions.

**Most Prevalent Method:** The **Committee Method** is the most prevalent in India because:
- It ensures tripartite representation (fairness)
- It is suitable for unorganized and heterogeneous sectors
- It can be customized to regional and sectoral variations
- It has statutory backing under the Minimum Wages Act

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Features of the Minimum Wages Act, 1948:**
- Applies to **scheduled employments** listed in the schedule (both agriculture and non-agriculture)
- Covers both **time-rated** and **piece-rated** workers
- Fixes minimum rates of wages for different categories of work
- Provides for **revision every 5 years** or less
- Appointment of **Inspectors** for enforcement
- Penalties for violation: imprisonment up to 6 months and/or fine up to ₹500 (first offence); stricter penalties for repeat offences

**Objectives:**
- To prevent exploitation of labour in unorganized sectors
- To ensure a minimum standard of living
- To protect workers' purchasing power
- To promote industrial peace by removing wage-related disputes

**Shortcomings:**
- **Limited coverage** — applies only to scheduled employments; many unorganized sectors are excluded
- **No uniform national minimum wage** — rates vary widely by state and sector
- **Weak enforcement** — shortage of labour inspectors
- **Inadequate penalties** — fines are too low to deter violations
- **Outdated norms** — the 1957 norms have not been comprehensively revised
- **Piece-rate complications** — determining minimum wages for piece-rated workers is complex

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Minimum Wage" with "Fair Wage"** — They are NOT the same. Fair wage is the middle tier; minimum wage is the floor. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "6 Norms"** — The six components of minimum wage (food, clothing, housing, fuel, education/medical, contingency) prescribed by the 15th Indian Labour Conference (1957) are **must-know** for KTU exams.
>
> 3. **Not Mentioning the 3,000 Calories Norm** — A very specific, high-yield point that examiners love to test.
>
> 4. **Mixing up the Acts** — Minimum Wages Act (1948) and Payment of Wages Act (1936) are different. Do not confuse them.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Revision Period** — Wage rates must be revised every **5 years**; this is a frequently asked 1-mark question.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Committee Names** — Mention the **Tripartite Committee (1948)**, **15th Indian Labour Conference (1957)**, and **National Minimum Wage** (Code on Wages, 2019) by name.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Minimum Wage**

- **Definition:** Lowest wage for bare subsistence + efficiency preservation (Statutory, Minimum Wages Act, 1948)
- **Three-Tier Structure:** Minimum → Fair → Living Wage (Committee on Fair Wages, 1948 / B.N. Adarkar Committee)
- **Six Norms (1957):** Food (3,000 cal/day) + Clothing (72 yds/yr) + Housing (5% of wage) + Fuel & Lighting + Education/Medical/Recreation + Contingency (15% of wage)
- **Base 1957 Wage:** ₹36 per month
- **CPI Adjustment Formula:** $W_{current} = W_{base} \times (CPI_{current} / CPI_{base})$
- **Theories:** Subsistence (Ricardo), Wage Fund (Mill: $W = F/N$), Marginal Productivity (Clark), Supply-Demand, Behavioural
- **Methods of Fixation:** Committee, Collective Bargaining, Wage Board, Legislation, Industrial Jurisprudence, Job Evaluation
- **Minimum Wages Act, 1948:** Scheduled employments, revision every 5 years, penalty up to 6 months imprisonment
- **Key Committees:**
  - Committee on Fair Wages (1948) — B.N. Adarkar
  - 15th Indian Labour Conference (1957) — 6 Norms
  - Code on Wages, 2019 (modern consolidation)
- **ILO Convention No. 131 (1970):** International framework for minimum wage fixation
- **Key Distinction:** Minimum Wage = Floor (legal), Fair Wage = Industry Capacity (negotiated), Living Wage = Aspiration (developmental)
- **Real-World Example:** Use fuel gauge analogy — Minimum = Empty Mark, Fair = Comfortable Fuel, Living = Full Tank

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick — "SHE FCE":** The six components of minimum wage:
> - **S**ubsistence food (3,000 cal)
> - **H**ousing (5% of wage)
> - **E**ducation/medical/recreation
> - **F**uel & lighting
> - **C**lothing (72 yds/year)
> - **E**mergency/Contingency (15% of wage)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fair Wage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET751 - PERSONNEL MANAGEMENT AND INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fair Wage # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Fair Wage** is a wage level that lies between the statutory **Minimum Wage** and the ideal **Living Wage**, determined by balancing two critical economic forces: (1) the **needs of the worker** (subsistence and a reasonable standard of living) and (2) the **capacity of the industry to pay** (financial viability of the employer). The concept was officially codified by the **Committee on Fair Wages** appointed by the **15th Indian Labour Conference (1957)**, a tripartite body consisting of representatives from government, employers, and trade unions. The Committee defined wages into three ascending categories. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Tripartite Definition (15th ILC, 1957)** > - **Minimum Wage**: Wage that must be paid irrespective of the industry's paying capacity — meant to sustain working efficiency. > - **Fair Wage**: Wage that is above the minimum but below the living wage — adjusted according to the industry's capacity to pay. > - **Living Wage**: Wage that maintains a decent standard of living — the ultimate social ideal. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **three-story building** representing the wage structure of an economy: | Floor | Wage Category | Analogy | |-------|---------------|---------| | **Top Floor** | Living Wage | A comfortable, spacious apartment with all modern amenities — the ideal lifestyle | | **Middle Floor** | **Fair Wage** | A decent, well-maintained apartment — comfortable but adjusted to the building's actual budget | | **Ground Floor** | Minimum Wage | A basic, modest room — meets only survival needs | The **Fair Wage** is like a **flexible elevator position** between the ground and top floor. It moves up or down depending on the **financial health of the industry** (the building's economic structure). A profitable IT company may place its workers closer to the top floor (near living wage), while a struggling handloom cooperative may keep them near the ground floor (near minimum wage), but **never below the legal minimum**. ## 1.3 Core Distinguishing Principle The uniqueness of a **Fair Wage** lies in its **elasticity and relativity**: $$\text{Minimum Wage} \;\leq\; \text{Fair Wage} \;\leq\; \text{Living Wage}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Fair Wage is a relative concept, not an absolute one.** Unlike the minimum wage (which is fixed by law) or the living wage (which is a social goal), the fair wage is **dynamic** — it changes with the industry, region, productivity, and economic conditions. ## 1.4 Historical Origin of the Concept The **Committee on Fair Wages (1957)** — also called the **Prasad Committee** (though popularly the Tripartite Fair Wages Committee) — laid down three key criteria for wage fixation: 1. **The worker's consumption needs** (food, clothing, housing, education, health) 2. **The industry's capacity to pay** (profitability, productivity, financial position) 3. **The prevailing rates of wages** in the same or similar occupations in the same region These three criteria are visualized in the **Fair Wage Triangle** discussed in later sections. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Conceptual Framework of Wage Classification The 15th Indian Labour Conference (1957) established a three-tier wage hierarchy. Each tier is governed by different economic logic: ### 2.1.1 Minimum Wage - **Purpose:** To prevent sub-human exploitation of labor - **Logic:** Based purely on the **subsistence needs of the worker and family** - **Determinant:** Set by the government through the **Minimum Wages Act, 1948** - **Capacity to Pay:** **Ignored** — industry MUST pay regardless of profitability - **Nature:** **Legal floor** — mandatory compliance ### 2.1.2 Fair Wage - **Purpose:** To balance worker welfare with industrial sustainability - **Logic:** Considers both **worker needs** AND **industry's capacity to pay** - **Determinant:** Determined through **collective bargaining, wage boards, and judicial adjudication** - **Capacity to Pay:** **Primary determinant** — wage rises when industry is profitable - **Nature:** **Equitable middle ground** ### 2.1.3 Living Wage - **Purpose:** To achieve a **decent standard of life** for the worker - **Logic:** Based on the **evolving social and cultural standard of living** - **Determinant:** An **aspirational goal** — not yet fully realized anywhere - **Capacity to Pay:** Assumed that **economic development** will eventually allow it - **Nature:** **Social ideal / target** ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Determinant** | **Status** | |-------------|--------------------------|-----------------|------------| | Minimum Wage | $W_{\min} = C_{\text{subsistence}}$ | Consumption needs only | Statutory (Legal) | | Fair Wage | $W_{\text{fair}} = f(C, P, M)$ | Capacity + Needs + Market | Equitable (Variable) | | Living Wage | $W_{\text{live}} = C_{\text{decent}}$ | Evolving standard of living | Aspirational (Ideal) | | Wage Range | $W_{\min} \leq W_{\text{fair}} \leq W_{\text{live}}$ | — | Inequality governing fair wage | | Real Wage | $W_{\text{real}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{nominal}}}{\text{Cost of Living Index}} \times 100$ | Inflation-adjusted | Purchasing power measure | > [!NOTE] > **Variable Legend:** $C$ = Consumption needs; $P$ = Industry's capacity to pay; $M$ = Prevailing market wage rate. ## 2.3 The Three Determinants of Fair Wage (Detailed) ### 2.3.1 (a) The Consumption Needs of the Worker This component considers: - **Subsistence** — food, shelter, clothing for the worker and family - **Health** — medical care, sanitation - **Education** — children's schooling - **Recreation** — basic leisure and cultural needs - **Insurance** — old age, accident, disability The Fair Wages Committee recognized that the **consumption needs differ by region** due to climate, dietary habits, and local cost of living. ### 2.3.2 (b) The Capacity of the Industry to Pay This is the **most distinguishing factor** of fair wage. It considers: - **Profitability** of the industry - **Productivity** of labor - **Financial reserves** and accumulated surplus - **Competitiveness** in the market - **Working capital** availability > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Principle:** A fair wage must NOT cripple the industry. If an industry cannot pay above the minimum wage, it should ideally restructure to improve productivity rather than suppress wages below the living wage indefinitely. ### 2.3.3 (c) The Prevailing Rates of Wages - Compares wages in **the same occupation in the same region** - Considers **similar industries** in comparable areas - Reflects **socioeconomic equilibrium** in the labor market - Prevents **unjustified disparities** between workers of similar skill ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Wage Policy The Fair Wage concept serves as the **operational compass** in: 1. **Wage Board Recommendations** — textile, sugar, cement, and other industries 2. **Industrial Tribunal Awards** — resolving wage disputes 3. **Collective Bargaining** — trade union negotiations 4. **Judicial Adjudication** — Supreme Court labor law pronouncements 5. **Corporate Compensation Policy** — multinational salary structures in India ## 2.5 Wage Determination Models There are three classical economic theories of wage determination that underpin the Fair Wage concept: | **Model** | **Theorist** | **Key Idea** | **Relevance to Fair Wage** | |-----------|--------------|--------------|----------------------------| | **Subsistence Theory** | David Ricardo | Wages tend toward subsistence level | Supports **minimum wage** floor | | **Wage Fund Theory** | J.S. Mill | Wages paid from a fixed fund | Explains **capacity to pay** constraint | | **Marginal Productivity Theory** | J.B. Clark | Wage = Marginal Revenue Product | Justifies **productivity-linked pay** | The Fair Wage framework **integrates all three models** — using subsistence as the floor, wage fund as the ceiling consideration, and productivity as the variable determinant. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Fair Wage Equation The **Fair Wages Committee (1957)** provided a logical framework that can be expressed mathematically to demonstrate how the three determinants interact. ### 3.1.1 Step 1: Define the Three Variables Let us denote the three core determinants: $$C = \text{Consumption Needs of Worker (Rs. per month)}$$ $$P = \text{Industry's Capacity to Pay (Rs. per month)}$$ $$M = \text{Prevailing Market Wage (Rs. per month)}$$ ### 3.1.2 Step 2: Establish the Wage Boundaries The absolute boundaries for any wage determination are: $$W_{\min} = \text{Minimum Wage (legally fixed)}$$ $$W_{\text{live}} = \text{Living Wage (aspirational target)}$$ The Fair Wage must necessarily satisfy: $$W_{\min} \;\leq\; W_{\text{fair}} \;\leq\; W_{\text{live}}$$ ### 3.1.3 Step 3: Formulate the Fair Wage Function The Fair Wage is a weighted function of the three determinants, constrained by the minimum and living wage boundaries. The Committee suggested: $$W_{\text{fair}} = \alpha \cdot C + \beta \cdot P + \gamma \cdot M$$ Where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \geq 0$ are weighting coefficients reflecting the relative importance of each factor in a given industry. ### 3.1.4 Step 4: Apply Boundary Constraints Substituting the boundary conditions: $$W_{\text{fair}} = \max\!\left(W_{\min}, \; \min\!\left(\alpha C + \beta P + \gamma M, \; W_{\text{live}}\right)\right)$$ This equation states that: - The fair wage **cannot fall below the minimum wage** (legal floor) - The fair wage **cannot exceed the living wage** (ideal ceiling) - Within this range, it is **weighted by the three determinants** ### 3.1.5 Step 5: Real Wage Conversion To express fair wage in terms of real purchasing power, divide by the cost of living index: $$W_{\text{real}} = \frac{W_{\text{fair}}}{\text{CPI}} \times 100$$ Where CPI = Consumer Price Index for the relevant region and base year. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement In a textile industry in Coimbatore (Tamil Nadu): - Statutory Minimum Wage = **Rs. 12,000/month** - Estimated Living Wage = **Rs. 30,000/month** - Worker Consumption Need (C) = **Rs. 15,000/month** - Industry Capacity to Pay (P) = **Rs. 22,000/month** - Prevailing Market Wage (M) = **Rs. 18,000/month** Weights: $\alpha = 0.4$ (needs), $\beta = 0.4$ (capacity), $\gamma = 0.2$ (market). ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Apply the formula:** $$W_{\text{fair}} = (0.4 \times 15{,}000) + (0.4 \times 22{,}000) + (0.2 \times 18{,}000)$$ **Step 2 — Compute each term:** $$0.4 \times 15{,}000 = 6{,}000$$ $$0.4 \times 22{,}000 = 8{,}800$$ $$0.2 \times 18{,}000 = 3{,}600$$ **Step 3 — Sum the components:** $$W_{\text{fair}} = 6{,}000 + 8{,}800 + 3{,}600 = 18{,}400 \text{ Rs.}$$ **Step 4 — Verify boundary conditions:** $$W_{\min} = 12{,}000 \;\leq\; 18{,}400 \;\leq\; 30{,}000 = W_{\text{live}} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Conclude:** The fair wage for this scenario is **Rs. 18,400/month**, which is above the legal minimum, reflects the industry's healthy capacity, and aligns with market rates — a balanced, equitable outcome. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Parameter** | **Minimum Wage** | **Fair Wage** | **Living Wage** | |---------------|------------------|---------------|-----------------| | Legal Status | Statutory (binding) | Equitable (variable) | Aspirational (target) | | Determined By | Government | Tripartite bodies / Wage Boards | Society (long-term goal) | | Capacity to Pay | Irrelevant | Primary consideration | Assumed achievable | | Realization Status | Fully enforced | Partially realized | Not yet achieved | | Economic Basis | Subsistence | Subsistence + capacity | Standard of living | | Volatility | Low (fixed) | High (industry-dependent) | Medium (gradual rise) | | Primary Function | Anti-exploitation | Balance & equity | Social upliftment | | Legal Backing | Minimum Wages Act 1948 | Industrial Disputes Act 1947 | Directive Principles (Art. 43) | | Adjustment Frequency | Periodic revisions by Govt. | Annual/Periodic negotiations | Long-term economic planning | ## 3.4 Fair Wage vs. Minimum Wage — Logical Deduction A clear logical distinction can be summarized as follows: > If **capacity to pay is zero** (e.g., a bankrupt firm), the **fair wage collapses to the minimum wage** — preserving the worker's survival while accepting the industry's inability to pay more. > If **capacity to pay is high** (e.g., a profitable MNC), the **fair wage approaches the living wage** — rewarding productivity and industry prosperity. This elasticity is what differentiates Fair Wage from a rigid statutory minimum. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Fair Wage Hierarchy (Pyramid Structure) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef living fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a2a44,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef fair fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef minimum fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef label fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 LW[Living Wage
Decent Standard of Living

when capacity is high"| LW MW -->|"never falls below

Viability]:::output A --> FW B --> FW C --> FW FW --> OUT1 FW --> OUT2 ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow of Fair Wage Determination ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef start fill:#70ad47,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef process fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef decision fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef result fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 S1([Establish Legal Minimum Wage]):::start S2([Estimate Living Wage Target]):::start P1[Assess Worker Consumption Needs]:::process P2[Evaluate Industry Capacity to Pay]:::process P3[Survey Prevailing Market Wages]:::process D1{Capacity to Pay
is Sufficient?}:::decision D2{Wage Exceeds
Living Wage?}:::decision R1[Fix Fair Wage above Minimum,
below Living Wage]:::result R2[Set Wage at Minimum Level]:::result R3[Cap Wage at Living Wage Level]:::result S1 --> P1 S2 --> P2 P1 --> D1 P2 --> D1 P3 --> D1 D1 -->|No| R2 D1 -->|Yes| D2 D2 -->|Yes| R3 D2 -->|No| R1 ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wage Determination System ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef input fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef engine fill:#ffe699,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef output fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef feedback fill:#f4b084,stroke:#c55a11,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 I1[INPUT BLOCK A
